You have learnt that matter consists of tiny particles called atoms. Take a closer look at the picture given above. Do you notice that both living beings, like... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 141 Journey I nside the Atom, 8.1 Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory, 8.2 A Short Historical Journey Through Atomic Models, 8.2.1 Thomson’s model of an atom. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Motion is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that journey Inside the Atom Chapter 8 Everything that you see or observe, or feel around is matter.

The text explains that you have learnt that matter consists of tiny particles called atoms.

The text explains that take a closer look at the picture given above.

The text explains that do you notice that both living beings, like us, and non-living things, like a house, are ultimately composed of atoms?

The text explains that these atoms are so tiny that they cannot be seen with the naked eye.

The text explains that you may be wondering — Is an atom truly the smallest unit of matter, or can it be divided even further?

The text explains that scientists, too, have been exploring whether atoms are divisible.

The text explains that if so, what are their constituents and how are these arranged?

The text explains that let us examine how the concept of atoms emerged and how it has been evolved since then.

The text explains that grade 8 Curiosity Chapter 7 Atoms Si N O H P C Human Body Organ Tissue Cell Silicates Brick Wall Room House Protein Cell Think It Over y Are atoms the smallest indivisible particles? y Why do electrons not fall into the nucleus even though they are attracted to protons in it? y Why did scientists keep modifying atomic models?

The text explains that 141 Journey I nside the Atom 8.1 Rediscovering the Roots of Atomic Theory Let us embark on a journey that takes us back more than 2,000 years, to the intellectual landscapes of ancient India and ancient Greece.

The text explains that in these distant yet remarkably parallel civilisations, profound thinkers, such as Acharya Kanada in India, and Leucippus and Democritus in Greece, pondered over the same fundamental question that has continued to inspire human inquiry across centuries — What is everything made up of?

The text explains that acharya Kanada suggested that if matter (dravya) is divided repeatedly, you will reach a stage where you would encounter the smallest particles that can no longer be divided.

The text explains that his ideas are recorded in the Sanskrit text Vaisesika Sutras.

The text explains that a parmanu is infinitely small and cannot be perceived by the senses.

The text explains that combinations of these forms dyads (groups of two parmanus) and triads (groups of three parmanus), and so on.

The text explains that it became the starting point for the current understanding of atomic structure.

The text explains that you may be wondering how Dalton’s idea evolved into a model for the structure of the atom.

The text explains that following Dalton’s theory, scientists were curious to know the answers of the questions: y What are atoms made up of ? y What would atoms look like if we could see them? y What makes the atoms of one element different from the atoms of another element?

The text explains that although we now know that the early models were not fully correct, they are still important because they show how science moves forward — one step at a time, driven by curiosity, questioning, and experimentation.

The text explains that thomson studied the conduction of electric current through gases at a very low pressure.

The text explains that later in this chapter, you will learn that an alpha particle is actually a nucleus of a helium atom containing two protons and two neutrons.

The text explains that 8.4: Schematic view of the gold foil experiment Beam of α-particles Thin gold foil Thomson’s model failed to explain the results of the gold foil experiment, particularly the deflection of some α-particles through large angles and that most of the α-particles passed undeflected.

The text explains that if you could ask Rutherford one question about his work, what would it be?

The text explains that 145 Journey Inside the Atom Rutherford’s atomic model was better than Thomson’s atomic model in explaining the results of the gold foil experiment.

The text explains that it could not explain the stability of the atom.

The text explains that limitations of Rutherford’s model While the idea of a central nucleus was a major step forward, Rutherford’s model could not explain why atoms are stable.

The text explains that as you have learnt in Chapter 4, Describing Motion Around Us, a particle moving in a circular path is constantly changing direction, which means it is accelerating.

The text explains that reason (R): According to Thomson’s model, electrons are embedded in a uniformly distributed positive charge sphere.

The text explains that he discovered the atomic nucleus and explained how some elements naturally break down, for which he won the 1908 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

The text explains that exploration|Grade 9 146 8.2.3 Bohr’s model of the atom To explain why atoms are stable, Niels Bohr proposed a new model of the atom in 1913.

The text explains that you may be wondering, how does Bohr’s model explain stability?

The text explains that bohr’s model could explain many experimental observations and marked a major step in understanding the atomic structure.

The text explains that he was curious about how atoms exist because the old models could not explain why electrons stay around the nucleus without collapsing.

The text explains that his explanation of the atomic structure provided more clarity.

The text explains that niels Bohr received the Nobel Prize in 1922, for his work on the structure of the atom.

The text explains that this also explains why atoms are heavier than the mass of their total number of protons.

The text explains that this breakthrough explained atomic mass and earned him the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1935.

The text explains that this has revealed key insights into materials like superconductors, battery electrodes, and drug molecules, helping develop better medicines, energy storage, and industrial alloys right here in India.

The text explains that 8.8: Bhabha Atomic Research Centre By 1869, scientists knew about 69 elements, most of which were found naturally on the Earth.

The text explains that 8.7.1 Building up atoms Let us create two-dimensional (2-D) atomic structures for various elements by adding one electron to the appropriate energy level each time atomic number is increased by 1.

The text explains that 8.11: Schematic atomic structure of the first eighteen elements showing how the electrons are filled in the K, L and M shells

The text explains that exploration|Grade 9 154 8.9 A Deeper Look into Atomic Structure 8.9.1 Isotopes Dalton proposed that all atoms of an element are identical and have the same mass.

The text explains that this raises an interesting question — should we consider the mass of a chlorine atom to be 35 u or 37 u?

The text explains that this is called a weighted average atomic mass, which can be calculated by multiplying the mass of each isotope by its percent relative abundance and then adding the two values, as explained below.

The text explains that as we end our journey into the structure of the atom, it is important to know that the story does not end here.

The text explains that the exploration of the structure of the atom is still being discovered (Fig.

The text explains that choose the correct options and explain the reason for the correct and incorrect options in the context of Ernest Rutherford’s gold foil experiment: (i) The experiment clearly showed the existence of neutrons in the nucleus.

The text explains that x Y Z Number of protons 18 17 17 Number of neutrons 19 18 20 Explain the relation between the following: (i) Y and Z (ii) Z and X 4.

The text explains that aman was discussing the structure of atom with his classmates.

The text explains that during the discussion, he learnt that an element X has a mass number of 35 and contains 18 neutrons.

The text explains that (vi) What will be the mass number if two neutrons are added to its nucleus?

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 8: Motion
